paper_title: Large-Scale Tunnel Air-Ground Collaboration With FLISP: Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Hydropower tunnel inspection is critical for infrastructure integrity yet remains inefficient and hazardous using manual methods. We propose FLISP (Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner), a mapless planning framework for cooperative UGV-UAV inspection. Unlike traditional map-based paradigms, FLISP features three core contributions: (1) a unified architecture where a single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU suite drives synchronized path generation for both platforms; (2) platform-specific solvers utilizing an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance and a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight; and (3) a hierarchical refinement strategy ensuring kinematic feasibility without state estimation drift. Benchmarks in a 1.2 km operational tunnel demonstrate that FLISP circumvents structural bottlenecks of map-based methods, eliminating map rasterization overhead (Fast-LIO2 + A*) and sampling instability (LIO-SAM + RRT*). FLISP achieves a 100% success rate with 7 ms latency, representing a 7-fold speedup over grid-based and a three-order-of-magnitude improvement over sampling-based baselines. Validated in operational hydropower tunnels, this approach offers a scalable solution for robotic inspection in feature-degraded linear infrastructure. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/Y_ezs1PfLJ4, and the code at https://github.com/ArchibaldGuo/FLISP.git.

ABSTRACT Hydropower tunnel inspection is critical for infrastructure integrity yet remains inefficient and hazardous using manual methods. We propose FLISP (Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner), mapless planning framework for cooperative UGV-UAV inspection. Unlike traditional map-based paradigms, FLISP features three core contributions: (1) unified architecture where single UGVmounted LiDAR-IMU suite drives synchronized path generation for both platforms; (2) platform-specific solvers utilizing an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance and dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight; and (3) hierarchical refinement strategy ensuring kinematic feasibility without state estimation drift. Benchmarks in 1.2 km operational tunnel demonstrate that FLISP circumvents structural bottlenecks of map-based methods, eliminating map rasterization overhead (Fast-LIO2 + A*) and sampling instability (LIO-SAM + RRT*). FLISP achieves 100% success rate with 7 ms latency, representing 7-fold speedup over grid-based and three-order-of-magnitude improvement over samplingbased baselines. Validated in operational hydropower tunnels, this approach offers scalable solution for robotic inspection in feature-degraded linear infrastructure. demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/Y ezs1PfLJ4, and the code at https://github.com/ArchibaldGuo/FLISP.git. INDEX TERMS Hydropower tunnel inspection, path planning, air-ground collaboration. I. INTRODUCTION ARGE-SCALE civil infrastructure, particularly hydropower water conveyance tunnels, constitutes critical backbone for sustainable energy management. Ensuring the structural integrity of these kilometers-long facilities is paramount. However, current maintenance relies predominantly on manual inspection, labor-intensive, hazardous process prone to subjective errors, thereby compromising long-term health assessments [1][5]. The adverse conditions within these confined spaces, characterized by poor lighting, homogeneous textures, and uneven terrain, necessitate automated solutions to enhance safety and data quality. Recent advancements in automation and robotics offer transformative approach to this long-standing industry problem [6][8]. While single-robot systems have shown promise, the scale and complexity of kilometer-long, higharched tunnels render them ineffective for comprehensive inspection. An unmanned ground vehicle (UGV) cannot access the tunnel crown, while an aerial vehicle (UAV) is limited by endurance. This necessitates collaborative, heterogeneous robotic system. However, deploying such systems in these GPS-denied, perceptually-degraded environments  (Fig. 1)  faces significant automation challenges: robust localization over long distances with repetitive textures, stable navigation on uneven or debris-covered surfaces, and real-time, collision-free path planning for multiple agents. Existing multi-robot planners, often derived from manufacturing or logistics, typically rely on structured environments or computationally expensive map-based frameworks (e.g., SLAM methods in the DARPA SubT Challenge [35]). These approaches are ill-suited for the linear, repetitive geometry of tunnels, where maintaining globally consistent map is both prone to drift and computationally prohibitive for routine inspection. Conversely, substituting global maps with purely reactive local planners avoids mapping overhead but renders the system highly susceptible to topological GUO ET AL.: LARGE-SCALE TUNNEL AIRGROUND COLLABORATION WITH FLISP FIGURE 1. The challenging operational environment of large-scale hydropower tunnel. Insets show typical textureless section (a), uneven silt accumulation (b), and structural obstacles like gates (c, d), underscoring the necessity for collaborative UGV-UAV system. deadlocks and kinematic inconsistencies in blind curves (detailed in Appendix A). To bridge this gap, we introduce the Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner (FLISP), novel framework for robust UGV-UAV tunnel navigation. Diverging from mapcentric paradigms, FLISP employs lightweight, mapless strategy. It leverages local geometry from single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU suite to generate synchronized, collision-free paths for the entire team in real time. This approach significantly reduces computational load and eliminates SLAM-inherent drift accumulation. We validate FLISP through extensive simulations and full-scale deployment in 1.2 km hydropower tunnel. Comparative analysis reveals the limitations of traditional map-based methods and highlights our approachs orders-of-magnitude efficiency gain. Distinct from loosely coupled multi-robot systems, FLISP establishes vertically integrated, asymmetric collaboration model. Instead of isolated planning, we centrally process environmental geometry on the UGV. This creates hierarchical dependency where the ground-based perception layer directly drives lightweight aerial path generation. Consequently, the system functions as unified pipeline, extending the robustness of the mapless philosophy from state estimation down to multi-agent control. Our primary contributions are: 1) hierarchical navigation framework using UGVcentric, asymmetric collaboration model, enabling real-time planning for UGV-UAV team using single sensor suite in degenerate environments. 2) Platform-specific, multi-objective obstacle avoidance strategies tailored to the unique challenges of constructed environments, such as UGV navigating on uneven, curved floors and UAV maneuvering around fixed infrastructure like sluice gates. 3) multi-level optimization mechanism that ensures path smoothness and computational efficiency, demonstrating robust pipeline from raw sensor data to executable trajectories. 2 To facilitate benchmarking, we release the source code and curated representative LiDAR-IMU dataset capturing critical geometric degeneracy in tunnels. Resources are available at: https://github.com/ArchibaldGuo/FLISP.git. II. RELATED WORK A. CLASSIC PATH PLANNING ALGORITHMS Path planning algorithms are broadly categorized into global reliant on prior environmental models (GIS paradigms, or static maps), and local paradigms driven by real-time perception [9], [10]. Global planning optimizes reference trajectories against topological, energy, and safety constraints [11]. Conversely, local planning focuses on online trajectory generation, utilizing sensor fusion for dynamic obstacle avoidance and kinematic feasibility [12]. Classical global algorithms include A* [13], Dijkstra [14], and RRT [15], while local approaches encompass Artificial Potential Fields (APF) [16], Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) [17], and deep learning techniques [18]. In the specific context of penstock and tunnel inspection, Ozaslan et al. [19] pioneered seminal framework for autonomous UAV navigation. Their work established critical methodology for standalone Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAV), demonstrating how onboard 3D mapping can effectively support localization and planning in these confined environments. While this map-centric approach is essential for single-agent operations, FLISP introduces collaborative UGV-UAV framework to address endurance limits and adopt mapless paradigm to mitigate the catastrophic mapping failures inherent to feature-degraded tunnels (as demonstrated in Section VI-C). Contemporary research increasingly integrates heuristic strategies to enhance robustness. For instance, HA-RRT [20] incorporates adaptive mechanisms to resolve RRTs slow convergence in maritime contexts. Similarly, hybrid algorithms combining honey badger optimization with cubic splines [21], and improved Q-learning with dynamic exploration [22], have demonstrated superior efficiency and convergence over traditional baselines. B. PATH PLANNING FOR MULTI-ROBOT SYSTEMS In recent years, research on path planning for multi-robot systems has increasingly focused on learning-based approaches to handle complex coordination. For instance, reinforcement learning frameworks, such as the Soft ActorCritic (SAC) algorithm for UAVs [23] and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) with experience replay [25], have been utilized to optimize obstacle avoidance and goal achievement. Similarly, imitation learning has been employed to enable agents to rapidly acquire complex behaviors from expert demonstrations [24]. In parallel with learning-based paradigms, traditional heuristic scheduling approaches, such as hybrid-state A* combined with conflict-based search [26], have been developed for multiple UGVs in warehouse logistics. However, these approaches predominantly target homogeneous agents and are strictly trained or evaluated in simulated, wellstructured grid environments. Consequently, they struggle to generalize to the non-convex, unmapped geometries and distinct kinematic constraints inherent to subterranean tunnels. C. PATH PLANNING FOR HETEROGENEOUS MULTI-ROBOT SYSTEMS For aerial-ground heterogeneous multi-robot systems, combining UAV and UGV capabilities significantly enhances joint mapping and planning. UAVs leverage their elevated vantage point for dynamic local mapping, which serves as the foundation for subsequent planning tasks [27]. Typically, UAV vision systems capture sequential imagery that undergoes rectification, stitching, and obstacle recognition to construct global ground map. Concurrently, UGVs employ 2D LiDAR to generate local feature sets. Based on these integrated maps, global and local planning are executed using A* and DWA algorithms, respectively. Similarly, Niu et al. [28] employ UAV-mounted wide-angle camera to localize multiple UGVs and obstacles, proposing concaveconvex programming algorithm to solve non-convex energy minimization problems for optimal UGV path generation. To address synchronization challenges, various architectures introduce intermediary agents or centralized nodes. Hu et al. [29] project 3D LiDAR data onto 2D occupancy grids constructed via UGV SLAM. They utilize an enhanced Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model for path planning, employing mediator agent to relay data and resolve conflicts between platforms. Li et al. [30] implement centralized data processing center acting as relay hub to facilitate seamless aerial-ground coordination. Sharing perception strategy similar to our work, Martinez-Rozas et al. [31] utilize LiDAR-generated local maps and an optimal RRT* method to simultaneously plan trajectories for UAV, UGV, and their connecting tether. However, the computational overhead of solving tether optimization equations significantly restricts performance. In field experiments, this resulted in stepwise movement patterns, highlighting the difficulty of achieving real-time agility in complex environments due to algorithmic complexity. D. METHODS IN TUNNEL ENVIRONMENTS Standard planning algorithms struggle in large-scale tunnels due to uneven terrain, GNSS denial, and geometric uniformity. In our previous work, we explored alternative methods with mixed success. Zhang et al. [32] utilized optical flow to mitigate UAV drift; however, this approach proves unreliable in low-light or curved sections and fails to address UGV attitude estimation. Similarly, Zhan et al. [33] implemented parametric cylindrical fitting methodology via LevenbergMarquardt optimization for incomplete point clouds. While geometrically accurate, its iterative nature incurs prohibitive computational costs for simultaneous multi-vehicle planning, and it neglects the critical roll-constraints of ground vehicles. Research on synchronous UGV-UAV tunnel planning remains scarce. Existing collaborative frameworks [34] often rely on the UGV maintaining global SLAM map to georeference the UAV. This dependency creates single point of failure: if SLAM drifts due to featureless geometry, the entire system collapses. Furthermore, state-of-the-art subterranean systems from the DARPA SubT Challenge [35][38] primarily target the exploration of complex networks via computationally intensive SLAM. Even frontier-based approaches that successfully bypass dense volumetric mapping [38] assume flat traversable surfaces, lacking the kinematic constraints essential for heavy UGVs navigating curved tunnel floors. Similarly, recent single-platform studies, such as those optimizing UGV navigation in mines [39], [40] or extracting UAV centerlines via occupancy grids [41] and vision [5], fail to address heterogeneous team synchronization. To summarize, existing methods typically depend on driftprone SLAM, assume flat ground traversability, or isolate platforms. FLISP bridges this gap by enabling simultaneous, mapless UAV-UGV path planning with platform-specific kinematic constraints tailored for geometrically degraded linear infrastructure. III. SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE A. FRAMEWORK AND CONTROL FLOW Fig. 2 illustrates the overall system architecture. Following initialization, gimbal-mounted tracking suite (LiDAR and RGB camera) monitors the UAVs relative position. Simultaneously, navigation LiDAR-IMU on the UGV provides environmental point clouds and attitude data. The UGVs path is generated via dynamic binning and multimodal fitting, switching to Firefly Algorithm (FA) for local avoidance upon obstacle detection. The UAV path is then derived hierarchically from the UGV path, adhering to altitude and communication safety constraints. cascaded control architecture executes these trajectories: outer-loop controllers transmit desired states to innerloop actuators, while continuous feedback from UGV local 3 GUO ET AL.: LARGE-SCALE TUNNEL AIRGROUND COLLABORATION WITH FLISP odometry (i.e., wheel encoders and IMU) and UAV positioning closes the loop. It should be explicitly stated that while FLISP does not require globally consistent map, it fundamentally relies on accurate local odometry from the UGV platform for short-term trajectory tracking. B. UAV AND UGV HARDWARE Fig. 3 depicts the hardware configuration. The aerial platform is DJI Mavic 3T, utilizing COTS controllers for low-level stabilization to offload trajectory generation. The custom UGV features GPU-accelerated industrial computer processing data from 64-line navigation LiDAR-IMU, UAV tracking module (comprising separate, short-range LiDAR and high-resolution monocular RGB camera on two-degree-of-freedom gimbal), and wireless infrastructure. Crucially, to accommodate the narrow clearance during vertical hoisting into the tunnel, the UGV employs heavyduty chassis with independently retractable wheel arms. This mechanical design, while robust, results in low steering response bandwidth that precludes rapid, large-angle rotations. The navigation LiDAR, which contains an integrated IMU, is mounted on the front-top of the UGV to maximize lookahead for curvature detection. While this creates nearfield blind spot, it poses negligible impact on FLISP. The planner extracts the global tunnel centerline via geometric fitting rather than relying on immediate terrain analysis. Furthermore, obstacle detection volumes (Section IV-A) are calibrated to effectively ignore this unmonitored region. The intrinsic integration of the IMU within the LiDAR assembly ensures synchronized state estimation. Operationally, the UGV serves as the primary surveyor and mobile base, while the UAV inspects the tunnel crown and structural anomalies inaccessible from the ground. C. COMMUNICATION TOPOLOGY Fig. 4 illustrates the communication architecture, designed as UGV-centric mobile network utilizing wireless bridge for long-range connectivity. Specifically, the UGVs onboard industrial router establishes local Wi-Fi zone for the UAV while maintaining wireless link to static mediating router at the tunnel entrance, which connects to the Ground Control Station via Ethernet. This dual-router configuration ensures robust, real-time data transmission over extended operational distances. Internally, the UGV computer functions as the integration hub: high-bandwidth sensors (LiDARs) and the router connect via Ethernet, while motion controllers and vision peripherals interface via RS-485 and USB, respectively. D. OPERATIONAL PHILOSOPHY FLISP is designed based on an Online Collection + Offline Reconstruction strategy. We adopt this approach because real-time SLAM algorithms frequently suffer from drift in featureless tunnels, which significantly compromises the accuracy of online defect localization. Instead, we utilize gimbal-mounted camera that captures images at fixed 4 frequency while rotating. By integrating this fixed visual sampling rate with the UGVs velocity, we perform 3D reconstruction offline. Crucially, this reconstruction logic necessitates strictly constant velocity and jitter-free trajectory to prevent alignment artifacts. Therefore, our algorithmic focus is explicitly placed on optimizing the spatial path geometry to ensure the smooth motion required for high-fidelity data acquisition, as evidenced by the complete tunnel model shown in Fig. 19. IV. FAST LIDAR-IMU SYNCHRONIZED PATH PLANNER This section details FLISP, designed for GNSS-denied tunnel environments. FLISP integrates three synchronized subsystems: (1) UGV-based state estimation and perception layer that serves as the computational core; (2) hierarchical planner that generates dynamically feasible trajectories for the UAV; and (3) robust control interface that handles this non-linear flight dynamics. As illustrated in Fig. 5, architecture ensures real-time responsiveness by decoupling heavy perception tasks from the lightweight execution layer of the UAV. The subsystems are linked via synchronized state machine, ensuring that the UAVs planning is continuously updated by the UGVs high-fidelity perception without latency-inducing global map consistency checks. For this work, we used only 3D LiDAR and IMU. This design choice is driven by three factors: First, LiDAR provides reliable geometric data in the low-light conditions typical of tunnels, where visual sensors often underperform. Second, direct geometric measurement is robust to the textureless tunnel surfaces that frequently cause failure in visual SLAM algorithms. Third, avoiding dense global mapping mitigates errors caused by structural homogeneity. Adding further sensors would increase system complexity and computational overhead, which aligns with the efficiency requirements of the proposed planner. A. TRAJECTORY PLANNING AND CONTROLLER key advantage of the FLISP framework is that it generates high-quality, kinematically aware paths directly from sensor data. This obviates the need for complex, computationally expensive trajectory optimization layers that are often required to smooth out paths from conventional planners. Consequently, we can employ standard, lightweight trajectory generation methods and simple controllers to effectively track the planned path. This section briefly outlines these implementations, which serve to validate the practical feasibility of the paths generated by FLISP. The overall control architecture is illustrated in Fig. 13. For both platforms, discrete waypoints from FLISP are converted into continuous, time-parameterized trajectories. For the UGV, we employ standard minimum-jerk trajectory generation based on quintic polynomial interpolation between waypoints, of the form: p(t) = (1 h1(t))p0 + h1(t)p1, (36) where h1(t) is the quintic polynomial basis function. To ensure feasibility, dynamic time reallocation mechanism is applied to respect the vehicles velocity and acceleration limits, following the approach in [42]. For the UAV, trajectory is generated using non-uniform B-spline representation, which ensures smoothness up to high derivative order. To mitigate excessive velocities at sharp turns, we incorporate curvature-aware time allocation mechanism by modulating time scaling factor λcurv: λcurv = 1.0 + kcurv(1.0 cos θ)p. where θ is the heading change angle, kcurv is gain parameter, and is the curvature sensitivity exponent, empirically set to kcurv = 8.0 and = 1.5. This ensures the UAV automatically slows down at corners. final time-scaling pass guarantees that all dynamic constraints are met. (37) Finally, feedforward Proportional-Derivative (PD) controller, standard choice for trajectory tracking, serves as the outer-loop controller for both vehicles. It computes velocity commands based on the errors between the current state and the desired state from the generated trajectory, which are then sent to the inner-loop controllers of the UGV and UAV. Limitations of Local and Uncoordinated Paradigms: To rigorously evaluate alternatives to SLAM-based mapping, we additionally benchmarked pure local planner (TEB on streaming point clouds). While successfully bypassing SLAM drift, it exposed three fundamental vulnerabilities inherent to uncoordinated 2D methods in our specific environment (detailed empirical analyses and visual proofs are provided in Appendix A): Local Occlusion Trap (UGV Straight-Line Artifact): Due to the inherent roof-shadow blind spot of UGVmounted LiDAR, cost-free void forms directly ahead. Pure local planners, optimizing for minimal immediate cost, invariably generate reactive straight-line path through this void. In curved segments, high-frequency execution of these straight lines actively drives the UGV toward the outer wall. FLISP circumvents this by performing macroscopic 50m polynomial fit, analytically guaranteeing an initial curvature-aligned arc that is impervious to local sensor blockages. Topological Deadlock under Roll Perturbation: Navigation relying on 2D costmap projections faces catastrophic edge case. If the UGV mechanically slips onto the curved sidewall, the 2D projection casts the surrounding walls as an impenetrable forward obstacle, isolating the vehicle from the valid center channel. Even with IMU-corrected orientation, 2D local planners become topologically deadlocked. FLISP inherently avoids this by integrating the IMU gravity vector into its global 3D geometric evaluation, synthesizing viable 3D recovery path back to the tunnel floor. VIII. CONCLUSION In this paper, we presented FLISP, collaborative planning framework designed to enable the automated inspection of large-scale, geometrically degenerate infrastructure. Addressing the limitations of traditional robotics in hazardous hydropower tunnels, we proposed lightweight mapless paradigm based on hierarchical polynomial fitting. By directly translating raw sensor streams from single LiDARIMU suite into safe, synchronized trajectories, our system effectively decouples path generation from global state estimation. This architectural shift mitigates the drift and consistency issues inherent to SLAM-dependent planners, providing robust solution for feature-poor conditions. The systems practical feasibility and efficiency were validated through comprehensive benchmarks and successful deployment in 1.2 km operational tunnel. The comparative analysis demonstrated that FLISP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art map-based baselines. While the grid-based baseline suffered from excessive memory consumption due to map rasterization and the sampling-based baseline failed to maintain real-time control frequencies, FLISP achieved 100% success rate with negligible latency and minimal resource footprint. Furthermore, empirical analysis confirmed that substituting our geometric approach with purely reactive local planners leads to unrecoverable topological deadlocks and synchronization failures. These results confirm our core hypothesis that for repetitive and resource-constrained environments, mapless approach is prerequisite for ensuring both system agility and operational stability. Future work will expand the frameworks versatility beyond linear structures by integrating topological decisionmaking layer for complex junctions. In contrast, FLISP (Burgundy) bypasses dense map updates, operating on sparse geometric features to achieve an order-of-magnitude latency reduction. expensive rasterization and inflation stages. This architectural advantage allows FLISP to maintain negligible latency, effectively decoupling planning speed from environmental resolution requirements. Resource Consumption and Real-time Feasibility: Beyond latency, the proposed framework exhibits minimal resource footprint, which is decisive for autonomous inspection missions. On SWaP-constrained onboard platforms, highfidelity data acquisition and high-frequency flight control already consume the majority of the computational budget. Consequently, the planning module must operate with negligible footprint to avoid process starvation or resource contention that could jeopardize stability. Method maintains an exceptionally low CPU Load (1.37%) and RAM Usage (39.7 MB), leaving ample resources for these concurrent mission-critical tasks. In sharp contrast, the map-based baselines incur heavy penalties: Memory Overhead: Both baselines require substantial memory. Method III consumes 1037.3 MB, demand dominated by the maintenance of the Global Occupancy Grid and obstacle inflation layers required for A* search, rather than the lightweight Fast-LIO2 estimator itself. This cumulative memory consumption poses severe risk of exhaustion during long-endurance exploration. Update Frequency Disparity: The computational bottleneck directly dictates the control bandwidth. Both Method (FLISP) and Method III (A*) are computationally efficient enough to saturate the set ROS publication frequency, ensuring continuous feedback control. However, Method II (Informed RRT*) is severely constrained by its sampling complexity. Statistical analysis reveals it operates at an average frequency of only 0.16 Hz. This implies the system executes in an intermittent open-loop fashion for extended durations ( 6 seconds per planning cycle), fundamentally 20 namic constraints requires rigorous low-level control. To this end, our recent parallel work [47] developed control barrier function (CBF)-based consensus tracking controller in simulation. Since FLISP generates the reference targets and the CBF controller ensures safety-critical execution, the two frameworks are highly complementary. Integrating this theoretically guaranteed controller with FLISP on the physical platform to evaluate their combined performance represents our immediate next step. APPENDIX: EMPIRICAL ANALYSIS OF LOCAL PLANNER To justify replacing reactive local planners with our macroscopic geometric approach, we benchmarked the Timed Elastic Band (TEB) algorithm against FLISP using identical LiDAR streams and safety parameters (e.g., 0.1m resolution, 0.7m inflation). This appendix evaluates their kinematic behavior, topological resilience, and computational efficiency in constrained 3D tunnels. A. The Heterogeneous Lookahead Paradox Tuning the lookahead horizon for 2D local planners exposes fundamental kinematic dilemma for heterogeneous teams. Short Horizon (5m): Limited lookahead produces jittery and unstable paths. For the UGV, this local path jitter translates into high-frequency steering control inputs, which the heavy chassis cannot physically execute, rendering the path unusable. Conversely, for the UAV, the projected aerial costmap is highly open, causing the planner to simply generate reactive straightline segments. Under continuous high-frequency replanning, these short straight segments piece together into jagged polyline, resulting in non-smooth and severely oscillatory flight. Long Horizon (10m): Extending the horizon to improve anticipation forces the optimization solver into conflicting soft constraints within the non-convex curved tunnel. To balance obstacle clearance and goal progression, TEB frequently compromises by generating abrupt mid-path kinks (Fig. 30, bottom). In sharp contrast to the high-frequency jitter of short horizons, these mid-path kinks subject the UGV to sudden, large steering control inputs, severely destabilizing its dynamic control. B. The Local Occlusion Trap (Roof-Shadow Blind Spot) Independent of the planning horizon, the UGV-mounted LiDAR inherently suffers from roof-shadow blind spot, creating perceived cost-free void directly ahead. To minimize optimization costs, TEB inherently tends to pull and straighten the trajectory through this void at the beginning of the planning cycle. While benign in straight sections, executing these rigid straight segments in curved pipes actively drives the UGV toward the outer wall, inevitably leading to rollover hazard. Notably, while SLAM-based methods eventually suffer from global map collapse in deep curves, their cumulative mapping effectively fills this physical void, thereby successfully avoiding this specific straight-line artifact prior to localization failure. FIGURE 30. Evaluation of TEB local planner in curved tunnels. The roof-shadow blind spot induces rigid straight-line planning at the start of the trajectory, driving the UGV towards the outer wall regardless of the chosen horizon. Top (5m): Short horizons cause uncoordinated and oscillatory UAV flight and high-frequency UGV control jitter. Bottom (10m): Extended horizons force soft-constraint conflicts, resulting in dynamically infeasible mid-path kinks. C. Topological Deadlock under Roll Perturbation Navigation relying on 2D costmap projections fails under 3D spatial disturbances  (Fig. 31)  . If the UGV slips onto the curved sidewall, the 2D projection artificially casts the surrounding walls as an impenetrable forward boundary. This pseudo-collision state forces standard 2D planners into an unrecoverable topological deadlock (marked by the red cross). Conversely, FLISP evaluates geometry natively in 3D. By integrating the IMU gravity vector, it synthesizes valid 3D recovery path back to the tunnel floor (magenta path), remaining completely immune to 2D projection artifacts. D.
